title: Conformal Prediction Assessment: A Framework for Conditional Coverage Evaluation and Selection
abstract: Conformal prediction provides rigorous distribution-free finite-sample guarantees for marginal coverage under the assumption of exchangeability, but may exhibit systematic undercoverage or overcoverage for specific subpopulations. Assessing conditional validity is challenging, as standard stratification methods suffer from the curse of dimensionality. We propose Conformal Prediction Assessment (CPA), a framework that reframes the evaluation of conditional coverage as a supervised learning task by training a reliability estimator that predicts instance-level coverage probabilities. Building on this estimator, we introduce the Conditional Validity Index (CVI), which decomposes reliability into safety (undercoverage risk) and efficiency (overcoverage cost). We establish convergence rates for the reliability estimator and prove the consistency of CVI-based model selection. Extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that CPA effectively diagnoses local failure modes and that CC-Select, our CVI-based model selection algorithm, consistently identifies predictors with superior conditional coverage performance.

sections.heading: 1Introduction
sections.text:
Predictive models based on artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning (ML) are increasingly deployed in high-stakes decision-making systems, ranging from healthcare and precision medicine (Topol, 2019 ) to criminal justice (Berk et al. , 2021 ) and financial risk assessment (Fuster et al. , 2022 ) . As the consequences of an erroneous prediction are typically borne by specific individuals rather than by the population on average, it is crucial to develop tools for reliable uncertainty quantification.
A growing body of literature emphasizes that despite achieving impressive average-level accuracy, modern models often exhibit substantial uncertainty heterogeneity across the feature space. Due to factors such as uneven data coverage, distribution shifts, or complex covariate interactions, this heterogeneity can lead to systematically overconfident or underconfident predictions for specific subpopulations (Chakraborti et al. , 2025 ) .

sections.heading: 1.1Uncertainty Quantification via Conformal Prediction
sections.text:
Conformal prediction (CP) addresses the challenge of uncertainty quantification by providing a rigorous, distribution-free and model-agnostic framework that yields valid predictive intervals with finite-sample marginal coverage guarantees under the assumption of exchangeability (Vovk et al. , 2005 ) .
The most widely adopted variant, split-conformal prediction, operates by partitioning the available data 𝒟 \mathcal{D} into a training set 𝒟 pred = { ( X i , Y i ) } i ∈ ℐ pred \mathcal{D}_{\text{pred}}=\{(X_{i},Y_{i})\}_{i\in{\mathcal{I}}_{\text{pred}}} and a calibration set 𝒟 calib = { ( X i , Y i ) } i ∈ ℐ calib \mathcal{D}_{\text{calib}}=\{(X_{i},Y_{i})\}_{i\in{\mathcal{I}}_{\text{calib}}} , with sizes n pred = | ℐ pred | n_{\text{pred}}=|\mathcal{I}_{\text{pred}}| and n calib = | ℐ calib | n_{\text{calib}}=|\mathcal{I}_{\text{calib}}| , respectively.
First, a predictive model μ ^ \hat{\mu} is fitted on 𝒟 pred \mathcal{D}_{\text{pred}} . Then, a non-conformity score function S ​ ( x , y ) := S ​ ( x , y ; 𝒟 pred ) S(x,y):=S(x,y;\mathcal{D}_{\text{pred}}) —typically the absolute residual | y − μ ^ ​ ( x ) | |y-\hat{\mu}(x)| —is evaluated on the calibration set.
For a user-specified miscoverage rate α ∈ ( 0 , 1 ) \alpha\in(0,1) , the prediction set for a new test point x ∈ 𝒳 x\in\mathcal{X} is constructed as: 𝒞 α ​ ( x ; 𝒟 ) = { y ∈ 𝒴 : S ​ ( x , y ; 𝒟 pred ) ≤ q ^ n calib , 1 − α } , \displaystyle\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}(x;\mathcal{D})=\{y\in\mathcal{Y}:S(x,y;\mathcal{D}_{\text{pred}})\leq\hat{q}_{n_{\text{calib}},1-\alpha}\}, (1) where q ^ n calib , 1 − α \hat{q}_{n_{\text{calib}},1-\alpha} denotes the ⌈ ( n calib + 1 ) ​ ( 1 − α ) ⌉ \lceil(n_{\text{calib}}+1)(1-\alpha)\rceil -th smallest value among the calibration scores { S i := S ​ ( X i , Y i ) } i ∈ ℐ calib \{S_{i}:=S(X_{i},Y_{i})\}_{i\in\mathcal{I}_{\text{calib}}} .
Assuming the calibration data and the test point ( X n + 1 , Y n + 1 ) (X_{n+1},Y_{n+1}) are exchangeable, split-conformal prediction guarantees marginal coverage : ℙ ​ ( Y n + 1 ∈ 𝒞 α ​ ( X n + 1 ; 𝒟 ) ) ≥ 1 − α . \displaystyle\mathbb{P}\left(Y_{n+1}\in\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}(X_{n+1};\mathcal{D})\right)\geq 1-\alpha. (2)

While the marginal guarantee is desirable, it is an average property taken over both the randomness of the calibration set and the test point. However, conformal prediction may have significant performance discrepancies across the feature space. This limitation motivates the pursuit of conditional coverage , a stronger property requiring validity for any given feature vector x x : ℙ ​ ( Y n + 1 ∈ 𝒞 α ​ ( X n + 1 ; 𝒟 ) ∣ X n + 1 = x ) ≥ 1 − α . \displaystyle\mathbb{P}(Y_{n+1}\in\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}(X_{n+1};\mathcal{D})\mid X_{n+1}=x)\geq 1-\alpha. (3) Although asymptotic conditional coverage is attainable under specific distributional assumptions (Lei and Wasserman, 2014 ; Lei et al. , 2018 ) , exact conditional coverage in a finite-sample and distribution-free setting is theoretically impossible (Vovk, 2012 ; Lei and Wasserman, 2014 ; Foygel Barber et al. , 2021 ) .
This theoretical barrier implies that, without additional assumptions, achieving exact conditional coverage is infeasible. Therefore, it is imperative to develop a sound approach for assessing the conditional coverage behavior of a CP algorithm based on the data at hand.

sections.heading: 1.2Our Contributions
sections.text:
To address the critical lack of rigorous tools for verifying conditional validity, we propose Conformal Prediction Assessment (CPA) , a general data-driven framework that transforms the problem of conditional coverage assessment into a concrete supervised learning task. By training a reliability estimator η ^ ​ ( x ) \hat{\eta}(x) to serve as a proxy for the unobservable conditional coverage probability, CPA enables a granular, instance-level examination of uncertainty models. Our specific contributions are threefold:

1. A rigorous, flexible and informative framework for evaluating conditional coverage. The reliability estimator η ^ ​ ( x ) \hat{\eta}(x) can leverage any powerful method of estimating the conditional probability function and thus overcome the limitations of traditional binning methods, allowing for scalable and objective assessment of conditional validity in high-dimensional feature spaces without requiring manual stratification. Beyond a single summary statistic, we decompose the conditional coverage assessment into Safety (undercoverage risk) and Efficiency (overcoverage cost), offering nuanced insights into model failure modes. We further introduce the Conditional Validity Profile (CVP) curve, a visualization tool that characterizes the full distribution of local conditional coverage probabilities. 2. Theoretical guarantees of consistency. We establish the convergence rates of the reliability estimator and prove that our proposed metric, the Conditional Validity Index (CVI), is a consistent estimator of the true conditional miscalibration. Furthermore, we characterize the trade-off between estimation and approximation errors, providing theoretical guidance for optimal data splitting. 3. Reliability-driven model selection (CC-Select). Departing from the standard focus on average interval length, we propose CC-Select , a robust algorithm for selecting models based on their conditional validity. We demonstrate through extensive experiments that CC-Select consistently identifies models with superior conditional coverage performance. In addition, the reliability estimators obtained during selection are retained and used to estimate conditional coverage at new inputs.

sections.heading: 2Methodology
sections.text: In this section, we introduce our data-driven framework for learning and predicting the reliability of any given prediction interval method. Figure 1 illustrates the entire pipeline. Details are provided in the following subsections.

sections.heading: 2.1Asymptotic Behavior of the Conditional Coverage Probability
sections.text:
In our proposed CPA scheme, estimating the conditional coverage probability function plays a central role. We next study the asymptotic behavior of the conditional coverage probability function under sensible conditions.
The finite-sample conditional coverage probability function is defined as η n ​ ( x ) = ℙ ​ ( Y n + 1 ∈ 𝒞 α ​ ( X n + 1 ; 𝒟 ) ∣ X n + 1 = x ) . \displaystyle\eta_{n}(x)=\mathbb{P}(Y_{n+1}\in\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}(X_{n+1};\mathcal{D})\mid X_{n+1}=x). (4) Here the probability is taken over both the training data 𝒟 \mathcal{D} and the future response Y n + 1 Y_{n+1} .
Consequently, η n ​ ( x ) \eta_{n}(x) is a deterministic function of x x , rather than a random quantity. Throughout the paper, { ( X i , Y i ) } i = 1 n + 1 \{(X_{i},Y_{i})\}_{i=1}^{n+1} are assumed to be independent and identically distributed and to follow the regression model Y = μ ​ ( X ) + ϵ Y=\mu(X)+\epsilon , where 𝔼 ​ [ ϵ ∣ X ] = 0 \mathbb{E}[\epsilon\mid X]=0 and the noise distribution may depend on X X .
Within this subsection, we focus on the absolute residual as the non-conformity score, i.e., S i = | Y i − μ ^ n pred ​ ( X i ) | S_{i}=|Y_{i}-\hat{\mu}_{n_{\text{pred}}}(X_{i})| . The corresponding prediction set is given by 𝒞 α ​ ( x ; 𝒟 ) = [ μ ^ n pred ​ ( x ) − q ^ n calib , 1 − α , μ ^ n pred ​ ( x ) + q ^ n calib , 1 − α ] \mathcal{C}_{\alpha}(x;\mathcal{D})=[\hat{\mu}_{n_{\text{pred}}}(x)-\hat{q}_{n_{\text{calib}},1-\alpha},\hat{\mu}_{n_{\text{pred}}}(x)+\hat{q}_{n_{\text{calib}},1-\alpha}] .

To characterize the convergence of η n ​ ( x ) \eta_{n}(x) , we need the following assumptions regarding the consistency of the regression estimator and the regularity of the error distribution.

Assumption (a) follows Lei et al. ( 2018 ) and serves as a baseline stability condition for μ ^ n \hat{\mu}_{n} . Typically, ζ n \zeta_{n} takes the form c ​ ( n / log ⁡ n ) − β c(n/\log n)^{-\beta} for some β > 0 \beta>0 , and ρ n \rho_{n} is of the order n − c n^{-c} for some constant c > 0 c>0 (where the choice of c c only affects the constant pre-factor of ζ n \zeta_{n} ). Note that we do not require μ ~ \tilde{\mu} to be the true regression function μ \mu . This accounts for practical scenarios where the regression model may be mis-specified; for instance, using a linear model to approximate a nonlinear regression function. In such cases, μ ~ \tilde{\mu} represents the best approximation of μ \mu within the model class under the sup-norm.

Theorem 1 ensures that as sample sizes increase, η n ​ ( x ) \eta_{n}(x) stabilizes towards a fixed deterministic function η ​ ( x ) \eta(x) . Crucially, however, this limit η ​ ( x ) \eta(x) is not necessarily equal to the target level 1 − α 1-\alpha everywhere. In particular, model misspecification (where μ ~ ≠ μ \tilde{\mu}\neq\mu ) or unmodeled heteroscedasticity can cause η ​ ( x ) \eta(x) to deviate significantly from 1 − α 1-\alpha , even
with a large n n . Since η n ​ ( x ) \eta_{n}(x) is a stable, learnable property of the model-data pair, we estimate it to evaluate the reliability of the given conformal method.

Figure 2 visualizes the asymptotic conditional coverage function η ​ ( x ) \eta(x) under several representative forms of model misspecification, illustrating that uniform convergence of η n ​ ( x ) \eta_{n}(x) does not generally imply convergence to the nominal target level 1 − α 1-\alpha .

sections.heading: 2.2Estimating the Conditional Coverage Probability Function
sections.text:
Consider a conformal prediction algorithm 𝒜 \mathcal{A} that maps a pre-specified level α ∈ ( 0 , 1 ) \alpha\in(0,1) , a training dataset 𝒟 0 {\mathcal{D}}_{0} and a new input x x to a prediction set 𝒞 α ​ ( x ; 𝒟 0 ) \mathcal{C}_{\alpha}(x;{\mathcal{D}}_{0}) . We emphasize that 𝒞 α ​ ( x ; 𝒟 0 ) \mathcal{C}_{\alpha}(x;\mathcal{D}_{0}) is a generic mapping, and 𝒟 0 \mathcal{D}_{0} will be allowed to vary in what follows. Our primary estimand of interest in this stage is the conditional coverage probability function η n ​ ( x ) \eta_{n}(x) in equation 4 . Since η n ​ ( x ) \eta_{n}(x) is unobservable, we adopt a sample-splitting strategy to estimate it. We partition the available full dataset 𝒟 \mathcal{D} into two disjoint subsets: a training set 𝒟 train \mathcal{D}_{\text{train}} and an evaluation set 𝒟 eval \mathcal{D}_{\text{eval}} , with n train n_{\text{train}} and n eval n_{\text{eval}} observations respectively. Let ℐ eval \mathcal{I}_{\text{eval}} denote the set of indices corresponding to the data points in 𝒟 eval \mathcal{D}_{\text{eval}} .

Step 1: Predictor Training. The set 𝒟 train \mathcal{D}_{\text{train}} is utilized to construct the conformal predictor 𝒜 \mathcal{A} . Note that 𝒟 train \mathcal{D}_{\text{train}} may require internal splitting based on the choice of 𝒜 \mathcal{A} . Specifically, for split-conformal methods, it is partitioned into a proper training set 𝒟 pred \mathcal{D}_{\text{pred}} (used to fit the underlying regression model μ ^ \hat{\mu} ) and a calibration set 𝒟 calib \mathcal{D}_{\text{calib}} (used to compute the empirical quantile of the conformity scores). Once trained on 𝒟 train \mathcal{D}_{\text{train}} , the predictor 𝒜 \mathcal{A} is fixed.

Step 2: Label Generation. We apply the fixed predictor 𝒜 \mathcal{A} to each data point ( X i , Y i ) (X_{i},Y_{i}) in the evaluation set 𝒟 eval \mathcal{D}_{\text{eval}} to generate prediction sets 𝒞 ^ i = 𝒞 α ​ ( X i ; 𝒟 train ) \hat{\mathcal{C}}_{i}=\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}(X_{i};\mathcal{D}_{\text{train}}) . We then construct binary coverage indicators I i I_{i} , which serve as the target labels for reliability: I i = 𝟙 ​ { Y i ∈ 𝒞 ^ i } , for all ​ i ∈ ℐ eval . \displaystyle I_{i}=\mathbbm{1}\{Y_{i}\in\hat{\mathcal{C}}_{i}\},\quad\text{for all }i\in\mathcal{I}_{\text{eval}}. (5)

Step 3: Reliability Learning. The problem of estimating η n ​ ( x ) \eta_{n}(x) is thus reduced to a supervised binary classification problem. We construct a new dataset 𝒟 eval ′ = { ( X i , I i ) } i ∈ ℐ eval \mathcal{D}_{\text{eval}}^{\prime}=\{(X_{i},I_{i})\}_{i\in\mathcal{I}_{\text{eval}}} and train a probabilistic classifier η ^ n eval \hat{\eta}_{n_{\text{eval}}} to minimize a strictly proper scoring rule (e.g., the cross-entropy loss). However, raw outputs from classification models are often not well-calibrated probabilities. To ensure the predictions more faithfully reflect empirical frequencies, we apply post-hoc probability calibration—specifically Isotonic Regression—to the classifier’s output. The resulting estimator outputs the predicted probability of coverage: η ^ n eval ​ ( x ) ≈ ℙ ​ ( Y n + 1 ∈ 𝒞 α ​ ( x ; 𝒟 train ) ∣ X n + 1 = x ) . \displaystyle\hat{\eta}_{n_{\text{eval}}}(x)\approx\mathbb{P}(Y_{n+1}\in\mathcal{C}_{\alpha}(x;{\mathcal{D}}_{\textup{train}})\mid X_{n+1}=x). (6)

Any probabilistic classifier capable of capturing nonlinear dependencies, such as random forests, gradient boosted trees (XGBoost), or calibrated neural networks, can be employed as the base learner for η ^ n eval \hat{\eta}_{n_{\text{eval}}} . The detailed training procedure is presented in Algorithm 1 in Appendix B , and the theoretical background on calibration is provided in Appendix C .

The implementation of Algorithm 1 involves three important practical choices: the split ratio, the number of splits, and the base learner. Regarding the data splitting ratio ρ ∈ ( 0 , 1 ) \rho\in(0,1) , defined such that n train = ⌊ ρ ​ n ⌋ n_{\text{train}}=\lfloor\rho n\rfloor and n eval = n − n train n_{\text{eval}}=n-n_{\text{train}} , a trade-off exists between the predictive power of the conformal predictor and the sample size available for reliability learning. Based on extensive simulations presented later, we adopt an even split of ρ = 0.5 \rho=0.5 as it consistently yields stable performance. Second, to mitigate the variance arising from random data partitioning, we employ a multi-split strategy by repeating the procedure over K K random splits and averaging the resulting estimates. We set K = 5 K=5 in our experiments, which effectively stabilizes the reliability estimate via aggregation. Finally, regarding the base learner ℒ \mathcal{L} , we select the best model from a candidate set via 5-fold cross-validation in our experiments. Importantly, our sensitivity analysis confirms that the framework is robust to this specific choice, provided the learner possesses sufficient capacity to capture nonlinear failure patterns and is coupled with the aforementioned isotonic calibration.

sections.heading: 2.3Assessment of Conditional Coverage
sections.text: With the trained reliability estimator η ^ n eval ​ ( x ) \hat{\eta}_{n_{\textup{eval}}}(x) , we can quantitatively assess the conditional coverage properties of the conformal predictor. While standard marginal coverage merely validates compliance with a global average constraint, our framework captures the underlying coverage heterogeneity , enabling a granular characterization of local reliability .

sections.heading: 2.4Reliability-Driven Model Selection and Deployment
sections.text:
A primary utility of the CVI metric is to facilitate objective model selection among a set of candidate conformal prediction procedures { 𝒜 m } m = 1 M \{\mathcal{A}_{m}\}_{m=1}^{M} . While traditional selection criteria focus on minimizing the prediction set size, our framework enables selection based on the fidelity of conditional coverage. We introduce a robust selection protocol, CC-Select , designed to identify the model with the lowest expected conditional miscalibration.

To mitigate the high variance associated with single-shot data splitting, CC-Select employs a repeated subsampling procedure. In each iteration, the dataset is randomly partitioned, and candidate models are ranked by their CVI scores. The procedure identifies the optimal model m ∗ m^{*} that minimizes the average CVI across all K K splits.

For the selected procedure 𝒜 m ∗ \mathcal{A}_{m^{*}} , the reliability estimators { η ^ m ∗ ( k ) } k = 1 K \{\hat{\eta}_{m^{*}}^{(k)}\}_{k=1}^{K} obtained from the repeated subsampling stage are retained. After model selection, we retrain 𝒜 m ∗ \mathcal{A}_{m^{*}} on the full dataset 𝒟 \mathcal{D} . For a new test input X new X_{\mathrm{new}} , we apply each stored reliability estimator to the selected procedure at X new X_{\mathrm{new}} and compute their average, c ^ ​ ( X new ) = 1 K ​ ∑ k = 1 K η ^ m ∗ ( k ) ​ ( X new ) , \hat{c}(X_{\mathrm{new}})=\frac{1}{K}\sum_{k=1}^{K}\hat{\eta}_{m^{*}}^{(k)}(X_{\mathrm{new}}), which serves as an estimate of the conditional coverage at X new X_{\mathrm{new}} . In applications, c ^ ​ ( X new ) \hat{c}(X_{\mathrm{new}}) can be compared to the nominal level 1 − α 1-\alpha to identify inputs for which the conformal prediction set may exhibit poor conditional coverage. A complete description of the two-stage procedure is provided in Algorithm 2 in Appendix B .

sections.heading: 3Theoretical Analysis
sections.text: In this section, we establish the theoretical foundations of the proposed Conformal Prediction Assessment (CPA) framework. First, we quantify convergence rates of the reliability estimator η ^ n eval \hat{\eta}_{n_{\textup{eval}}} , explicitly characterizing the trade-off between estimation and approximation errors introduced by sample splitting. Building on this pointwise convergence, we demonstrate the consistency of the Conditional Validity Index (CVI) as a reliable proxy for true conditional miscalibration. Finally, we provide rigorous guarantees for the model selection procedure, establishing its consistency in identifying the optimal conformal predictor.

sections.heading: 3.1Convergence of Conditional Coverage Estimates
sections.text:
We now analyze the convergence properties of our estimator η ^ n eval ​ ( x ) \hat{\eta}_{n_{\textup{eval}}}(x) , which is fitted on the evaluation data { ( X i , I i ) } i ∈ ℐ eval \{(X_{i},I_{i})\}_{i\in\mathcal{I}_{\textup{eval}}} . Conditional on the feature X i X_{i} , the coverage indicators I i I_{i} are independent Bernoulli random variables: I i ∣ X i ∼ Bernoulli ​ ( η n train ​ ( X i ) ) , \displaystyle I_{i}\mid X_{i}\sim\text{Bernoulli}(\eta_{n_{\textup{train}}}(X_{i})), (14) where η n train ​ ( x ) := ℙ ​ ( Y ∈ 𝒞 ​ ( X ; 𝒟 train ) ∣ X = x ) \eta_{n_{\textup{train}}}(x):=\mathbb{P}(Y\in\mathcal{C}(X;\mathcal{D}_{\textup{train}})\mid X=x) denotes the conditional coverage function associated with a conformal prediction model trained with sample size n train n_{\textup{train}} .

Our objective is to bound the deviation ‖ η ^ n eval − η n ‖ ∞ \|\hat{\eta}_{n_{\textup{eval}}}-\eta_{n}\|_{\infty} , which quantifies the uniform error of our reliability estimator relative to the true conditional coverage function η n ​ ( x ) \eta_{n}(x) for the full dataset 𝒟 \mathcal{D} . By the triangle inequality, we bound this error by the sum of two components: ‖ η ^ n eval − η n ‖ ∞ ≤ ‖ η ^ n eval − η n train ‖ ∞ ⏟ Estimation Error + ‖ η n train − η n ‖ ∞ ⏟ Approximation Error . \displaystyle\|\hat{\eta}_{n_{\textup{eval}}}-\eta_{n}\|_{\infty}\leq\underbrace{\|\hat{\eta}_{n_{\textup{eval}}}-\eta_{n_{\textup{train}}}\|_{\infty}}_{\text{Estimation Error}}+\underbrace{\|\eta_{n_{\textup{train}}}-\eta_{n}\|_{\infty}}_{\text{Approximation Error}}. (15) The Estimation Error arises from estimating the function η n train \eta_{n_{\textup{train}}} using a finite evaluation set 𝒟 eval \mathcal{D}_{\textup{eval}} . The Approximation Error accounts for the discrepancy between the coverage of the model trained on the subsample 𝒟 train \mathcal{D}_{\textup{train}} and that of the model trained on the full dataset 𝒟 \mathcal{D} . To bound these terms, we introduce the following assumption regarding the classifier’s convergence rate.

This assumption is standard in nonparametric classification. For instance, kernel regression estimators typically achieve a rate of ψ n ≍ ( n / log ⁡ n ) − γ \psi_{n}\asymp(n/\log n)^{-\gamma} for some γ > 0 \gamma>0 , under suitable regularity conditions (Stone, 1982 ) .

Next, to bound the Approximation Error ‖ η n train − η n ‖ ∞ \|\eta_{n_{\textup{train}}}-\eta_{n}\|_{\infty} , we require the finite-sample conditional coverage function to stabilize towards a fixed deterministic limit as the sample size increases. This stability ensures that the coverage properties learned from the subsample 𝒟 train \mathcal{D}_{\textup{train}} remain representative of the full model’s behavior. We formalize this requirement as follows:

The validity of this assumption is grounded in our earlier asymptotic analysis. Specifically, Theorem 1 establishes that for split-conformal prediction with absolute residual scores, this stability condition holds with a rate of φ n = O ​ ( ζ n pred + ρ n pred + exp ⁡ ( − c ​ n calib ​ ζ n pred 2 ) ) \varphi_{n}=O(\zeta_{n_{\textup{pred}}}+\rho_{n_{\text{pred}}}+\exp(-cn_{\textup{calib}}\zeta_{n_{\textup{pred}}}^{2})) . By formulating this property as a general assumption, we decouple the consistency analysis of our reliability estimator from the specific mechanics of the base CP algorithm. Consequently, our framework remains applicable to any uncertainty quantification method that exhibits asymptotic stability.

Since n train < n n_{\textup{train}}<n , the rate φ n train \varphi_{n_{\textup{train}}} typically dominates φ n \varphi_{n} , rendering the overall rate effectively O p ​ ( ψ n eval + φ n train ) O_{p}(\psi_{n_{\textup{eval}}}+\varphi_{n_{\textup{train}}}) . This result highlights a fundamental trade-off governed by the data split: increasing the evaluation set size n eval n_{\textup{eval}} reduces the estimation error rate ψ \psi , but simultaneously shrinks the training set n train n_{\textup{train}} , thereby increasing the approximation error rate φ \varphi . This theoretical insight motivates the use of a balanced split ratio to effectively manage these competing sources of error.

sections.heading: 3.2Consistency of the CVI Metric
sections.text:
Having established pointwise convergence of the reliability estimator, we now turn our analysis to the Conditional Validity Index (CVI). As a global summary statistic, the CVI aggregates local coverage deviations to quantify overall conditional miscalibration of the predictor.

To rigorously analyze consistency, we define the population-level counterpart to our empirical metric. Let the Oracle CVI be the expected absolute deviation of the true conditional coverage probability η n ​ ( X ) \eta_{n}(X) from the target level 1 − α 1-\alpha , over the feature distribution P X P_{X} : CVI oracle , n = 𝔼 X ∼ P X ​ [ | η n ​ ( X ) − ( 1 − α ) | ] . \displaystyle\textup{CVI}_{\textup{oracle},n}=\mathbb{E}_{X\sim P_{X}}\left[\left|\eta_{n}(X)-(1-\alpha)\right|\right]. (16) This quantity represents the ideal, unobservable measure of conditional miscalibration for the trained model.

The following theorem establishes that the empirical CVI, computed on the evaluation set, is a consistent estimator of this Oracle CVI. This confirms that the metric calculated from data converges in probability to the true underlying performance characteristic.

This consistency result provides the theoretical foundation for using CVI as a benchmark metric in practice. It ensures that with sufficient data, the empirical assessment faithfully reflects the true conditional reliability, thereby enabling valid comparisons and rankings of different conformal prediction models.

sections.heading: 3.3Consistency of Model Selection via CVI
sections.text:
Finally, we investigate the consistency of model selection based on CVI. That is, we analyze whether CVI can consistently identify the algorithm with the superior conditional coverage performance. To formalize this, we consider two competing conformal prediction algorithms, indexed by k ∈ { 1 , 2 } k\in\{1,2\} . Their respective empirical and Oracle CVI metrics are defined as: CVI n eval ( k ) \displaystyle\text{CVI}_{n_{\textup{eval}}}^{(k)} = 1 n eval ​ ∑ i ∈ ℐ eval | η ^ n eval ( k ) ​ ( X i ) − ( 1 − α ) | , \displaystyle=\frac{1}{n_{\textup{eval}}}\sum_{i\in\mathcal{I}_{\text{eval}}}\left|\hat{\eta}_{n_{\textup{eval}}}^{(k)}(X_{i})-(1-\alpha)\right|, CVI oracle , n ( k ) \displaystyle\text{CVI}_{\textup{oracle},n}^{(k)} = 𝔼 X ∼ P X ​ | η n ( k ) ​ ( X ) − ( 1 − α ) | . \displaystyle=\mathbb{E}_{X\sim P_{X}}|\eta_{n}^{(k)}(X)-(1-\alpha)|. Under Assumption 3 , we have ‖ η n ( k ) − η ( k ) ‖ ∞ = o p ​ ( 1 ) \|\eta_{n}^{(k)}-\eta^{(k)}\|_{\infty}=o_{p}(1) , where η ( k ) \eta^{(k)} is the deterministic asymptotic coverage function for k = 1 , 2 k=1,2 , respectively. This implies that the oracle CVI also converges to a deterministic limit: CVI oracle , n ( k ) → 𝑝 𝔼 X ∼ P X | η ( k ) ( X ) − ( 1 − α ) | = : CVI oracle ( k ) . \text{CVI}_{\textup{oracle},n}^{(k)}\xrightarrow{p}\mathbb{E}_{X\sim P_{X}}|\eta^{(k)}(X)-(1-\alpha)|=:\text{CVI}_{\textup{oracle}}^{(k)}. A challenging scenario arises when both algorithms achieve perfect asymptotic coverage, i.e., CVI oracle ( 1 ) = CVI oracle ( 2 ) = 0 \textup{CVI}_{\textup{oracle}}^{(1)}=\textup{CVI}_{\textup{oracle}}^{(2)}=0 . In this case, their finite-sample performance, dictated by their convergence rates, becomes the deciding factor. We introduce the following definition to compare their rates.

We now present the main theorem on the consistency of model selection.

The additional condition in Case 2 is standard in the model selection literature (Yang, 2007 ) . It ensures that the estimation error of the empirical CVI is asymptotically negligible compared to the Oracle CVI of the worse model. The numerator represents the estimation error rate. Under Assumption 2 , ‖ η ^ n eval ( k ) − η n train ( k ) ‖ ∞ = O p ​ ( ψ n eval ) \|\hat{\eta}_{n_{\textup{eval}}}^{(k)}-\eta_{n_{\textup{train}}}^{(k)}\|_{\infty}=O_{p}(\psi_{n_{\textup{eval}}}) . The denominator, CVI oracle , n train ( 2 ) \text{CVI}_{\textup{oracle},n_{\textup{train}}}^{(2)} , reflects the rate of convergence of algorithm 2, as CVI oracle , n train ( 2 ) = 𝔼 X ∼ P X ​ | η n train ( 2 ) ​ ( X ) − η ( 2 ) ​ ( X ) | = O p ​ ( φ n train ) \text{CVI}_{\textup{oracle},n_{\textup{train}}}^{(2)}=\mathbb{E}_{X\sim P_{X}}|\eta_{n_{\textup{train}}}^{(2)}(X)-\eta^{(2)}(X)|=O_{p}(\varphi_{n_{\textup{train}}}) . The condition thus requires that the estimation error vanishes faster than the true conditional coverage rate of the worse model.

sections.heading: 4Simulation Studies
sections.text: We conduct a comprehensive simulation study to evaluate the CPA framework’s efficacy across diverse distributional scenarios, with three primary objectives: (1) to verify that estimated conditional validity recovers ground-truth performance; (2) to assess robustness to reliability estimator specification; and (3) to provide empirical deployment guidance. To this end, we first validate ranking fidelity, distributional recovery, and robustness across four synthetic scenarios (Sections 4.2 – 4.3 ). We then examine operational prerequisites—data allocation and sample complexity—to derive real-world guidelines (supported by Appendices I – J and synthesized in Section 4.4 ).

sections.heading: 4.2Predictive Fidelity of Model Selection
sections.text: The utility of CPA hinges on its ability to minimize the selection-deployment gap. To quantify this, we assess the rank concordance between the CPA-estimated ranking π ^ \hat{\pi} (based on estimated CVI) and the ground-truth Oracle ranking π oracle \pi^{\text{oracle}} . We employ three complementary metrics: Weighted Kendall’s τ w \tau_{w} (global correlation), Hit@k (top- k k precision), and NDCG (selection utility).

sections.heading: 4.3Robustness to Reliability Estimator Misspecification
sections.text:
A critical consideration for practical deployment is the sensitivity of assessment outcomes to the specification of the reliability estimator η ^ \hat{\eta} . To systematically investigate this, we benchmarked our recommended AutoML Baseline (an ensemble with Isotonic Calibration) against six perturbations designed to probe specific failure modes. These range from uncalibrated variants to models subject to severe structural misspecification (see Appendix G for detailed specifications).

Figure 5 visualizes the impact of these perturbations on ranking fidelity. The results reveal a distinct bifurcation in performance metrics: • Stability of Selection Utility: While CPA consistently identifies top models (high NDCG@1), the degradation of Logistic Regression in nonlinear regimes (e.g., Setting B) highlights the necessity of sufficient model capacity for complex boundaries. • Monotonicity Drives Selection: The similar NDCG in most settings between the Baseline and Uncalibrated configurations implies that monotonicity is the primary determinant of selection utility. While strict probabilistic calibration remains requisite for accurate absolute auditing, it is secondary for the relative ordering of candidates.

However, a distinct failure mode is identified in the nonlinear Setting B, where the Logistic Regression estimator exhibits a severe breakdown in rank correlation. As detailed in the bias analysis in Appendix H , this failure is attributable to structural misspecification : the linear decision boundary lacks the flexibility to model the complex level sets of the true conditional coverage probability. This highlights a key prerequisite: the reliability estimator must have sufficient model complexity .

sections.heading: 4.4Discussion of Simulation Results
sections.text: The simulation studies validate CPA as a robust diagnostic instrument for auditing uncertainty quantification, demonstrating the CVI metric and CVP curves as consistent estimators of ground-truth performance. Based on these findings, we recommend employing an AutoML-based ensemble with Isotonic Calibration as the default reliability estimator for robustness to model mismatch. Regarding data allocation, our sensitivity analysis (detailed in Appendix I ) indicates that a balanced split ratio ( ρ = 0.5 \rho=0.5 ) mitigates the trade-off between approximation and estimation errors. Furthermore, reliable auditing requires sufficient data to learn the failure boundary; our empirical investigation suggests a minimum sample size of N ≈ 800 N\approx 800 for standard regression tasks (see Appendix J ).

sections.heading: 5.2Reliability Checking
sections.text:
We assess the calibration of the reliability estimator η ^ ​ ( x ) \hat{\eta}(x) to ensure that the predicted probabilities accurately reflect true coverage frequencies. We employ two standard diagnostics: Reliability Diagrams , which visualize the alignment between predicted probability and empirical coverage relative to the identity line y = x y=x , and the Expected Calibration Error (ECE) , which quantifies the weighted average absolute deviation from perfect calibration. The formal definitions and detailed methodology are provided in Appendix C .

Results. Figure 6 shows that η ^ ​ ( x ) \hat{\eta}(x) is well calibrated across all nine datasets: empirical coverage closely matches predicted reliability with ECE values typically below 0.01. This supports using CPA to audit conformal predictors and validates η ^ ​ ( x ) \hat{\eta}(x) as a practical proxy for conditional coverage.

sections.heading: 5.3Model Selection
sections.text: We assess the efficacy of the CC-Select algorithm (Algorithm 2 ) in identifying conformal prediction procedures that optimize conditional coverage.

sections.heading: 5.4Diagnosing Heterogeneity in Bike Sharing Demand
sections.text:
This case study illustrates CPA’s capacity as a diagnostic tool to uncover covariate-dependent prediction failure modes, which are obscured by marginal metrics. We focus on the Bike dataset, where the target is the hourly rental count, and covariates include temporal, calendar, and weather variables. We apply the nested protocol (Section 5.1 ) and examine three representative conformal procedures: CP-Residual, CP-Studentized, and CQR. We also conduct experiments examining interactions between two covariates, as detailed in Appendix K.3 .

We examine how conditional coverage η ^ ​ ( x ) \hat{\eta}(x) varies along individual covariates. Figure 8 (top) plots the estimated coverage as a function of the hour of the day. Although all methods achieve near-nominal marginal coverage, heterogeneity is pronounced: the residual-based method exhibits noticeable undercoverage during the commuting peaks (8–9am and 5–6pm), a high-volatility regime where the homoscedastic residual model is misspecified. In contrast, CQR maintains coverage closer to the nominal level during these periods. A similar analysis for temperature (Figure 8 , bottom) shows that while coverage is satisfactory at moderate temperatures, residual-based methods tend to over-cover at temperature extremes, while CQR remains closer to the nominal level. These univariate analyses expose systematic coverage deficits in specific regions (e.g., rush hours) invisible from global metrics.

sections.heading: 6Conclusion
sections.text:
We have introduced Conformal Prediction Assessment (CPA), a unified framework for evaluating and comparing the conditional coverage behaviors of conformal prediction methods. By formulating conditional validity assessment as a supervised learning problem, CPA enables scalable, instance-level auditing of coverage reliability beyond marginal guarantees.

CPA is built around a learned reliability estimator that approximates the conditional coverage function. This estimator underlies the Conditional Validity Index (CVI), which, together with a decomposition into safety and efficiency components, provides an interpretable summary of conditional miscalibration. We establish convergence of the reliability estimator, consistency of the CVI, and asymptotic validity of CVI-based model selection, grounding the proposed assessment in a sound theoretical framework.

Practically, CPA offers a principled alternative to binning-based diagnostics and demonstrates how conditional validity can guide model selection through the CC-Select procedure. The learned reliability scores may also be used as deployment-time indicators for identifying regions of unreliable uncertainty quantification.

Overall, CPA formalizes conditional coverage as a learnable object, enabling systematic evaluation and selection of conformal prediction methods, with direct implications for reliable deployment in heterogeneous settings.

sections.heading: C.1The Role and Scope of Calibration in CPA
sections.text:
Calibration is a fundamental validity property for probability forecasts (Dawid, 1982 ) . Let I ∈ { 0 , 1 } I\in\{0,1\} denote the coverage indicator in CPA, and let g : 𝒳 → [ 0 , 1 ] g:\mathcal{X}\to[0,1] be any probabilistic predictor for I I . The estimator g g is defined as perfectly calibrated if ℙ ​ ( I = 1 ∣ g ​ ( X ) = p ) = p , ∀ p ∈ [ 0 , 1 ] ​ (a.s.) . \mathbb{P}(I=1\mid g(X)=p)=p,\qquad\forall p\in[0,1]\ \text{(a.s.)}. (20) Conceptually, among all samples assigned a predicted probability of p p , the empirical frequency of true coverage should exactly equal p p .

Necessity for CVI. CVI aggregates the absolute deviations | η ^ ​ ( X ) − ( 1 − α ) | |\hat{\eta}(X)-(1-\alpha)| , and the resulting trust score operates as an absolute probability of coverage. Consequently, systematic over- or under-confidence in η ^ \hat{\eta} can severely distort the magnitude of these metrics, even if the estimator possesses excellent ranking performance.

Calibration vs. Resolution. Importantly, perfect calibration alone does not guarantee that g ​ ( x ) g(x) accurately recovers the true heterogeneity of η ​ ( x ) \eta(x) . For instance, a naive marginal predictor g ​ ( x ) ≡ 𝔼 ​ [ I ] g(x)\equiv\mathbb{E}[I] is perfectly calibrated but completely fails to capture variations across the feature space. Therefore, the efficacy of CPA hinges on two complementary properties: (i) the resolution (predictive capacity) of the base model to capture local coverage heterogeneity, and (ii) proper calibration to ensure these probabilities are numerically interpretable and comparable across candidate methods.

sections.heading: C.2Post-Hoc Calibration Procedure
sections.text:
High-capacity learners, such as tree ensembles or neural networks, can achieve high classification accuracy yet produce notoriously miscalibrated probability outputs (Niculescu-Mizil and Caruana, 2005 ) . To rectify this, we apply a post-hoc calibration step to the raw probability predictor f ^ ​ ( x ) \hat{f}(x) trained on { ( X i , I i ) } \{(X_{i},I_{i})\} . This is achieved by learning a monotonically increasing mapping h : [ 0 , 1 ] → [ 0 , 1 ] h:[0,1]\to[0,1] , yielding the final calibrated estimator η ^ ​ ( x ) = h ​ ( f ^ ​ ( x ) ) \hat{\eta}(x)=h(\hat{f}(x)) .

Isotonic Regression. We adopt isotonic regression (Zadrozny and Elkan, 2002 ) as our primary calibration technique due to its non-parametric flexibility and model-agnostic nature. Given a calibration subset ℐ cal \mathcal{I}_{\mathrm{cal}} (strictly disjoint from the data used to fit f ^ \hat{f} ), we solve: h ^ iso = arg ⁡ min h ​ non-decreasing ​ ∑ i ∈ ℐ cal ( I i − h ​ ( f ^ ​ ( X i ) ) ) 2 . \hat{h}_{\mathrm{iso}}=\arg\min_{h\ \text{non-decreasing}}\sum_{i\in\mathcal{I}_{\mathrm{cal}}}\bigl(I_{i}-h(\hat{f}(X_{i}))\bigr)^{2}. (21) To prevent overfitting and data leakage, particularly when sample sizes are constrained, we implement this calibration via a cross-fitting strategy within 𝒟 eval \mathcal{D}_{\mathrm{eval}} .

Parametric Alternatives. While parametric approaches like Platt scaling or temperature scaling (Platt, 1999 ; Guo et al. , 2017 ) are viable alternatives, isotonic regression is preferred in our pipeline. It robustly accommodates the diverse array of base learners utilized in our AutoML reliability search without imposing restrictive distributional assumptions.

sections.heading: C.3Diagnostic Metrics: Reliability Diagrams and ECE
sections.text: We empirically assess calibration quality using reliability diagrams and the Expected Calibration Error (ECE), adhering to standard conventions (Guo et al. , 2017 ) . For a set of test samples { ( X j , I j ) } j = 1 n \{(X_{j},I_{j})\}_{j=1}^{n} , we partition the predictions into K K equal-frequency bins { B 1 , … , B K } \{B_{1},\dots,B_{K}\} based on the estimated probabilities η ^ ​ ( X j ) \hat{\eta}(X_{j}) . For each bin B k B_{k} , the mean predicted confidence and empirical accuracy are computed as: conf ​ ( B k ) = 1 | B k | ​ ∑ j ∈ B k η ^ ​ ( X j ) , acc ​ ( B k ) = 1 | B k | ​ ∑ j ∈ B k I j . \mathrm{conf}(B_{k})=\frac{1}{|B_{k}|}\sum_{j\in B_{k}}\hat{\eta}(X_{j}),\qquad\mathrm{acc}(B_{k})=\frac{1}{|B_{k}|}\sum_{j\in B_{k}}I_{j}. (22) The reliability diagram plots ( conf ​ ( B k ) , acc ​ ( B k ) ) (\mathrm{conf}(B_{k}),\mathrm{acc}(B_{k})) against the ideal identity line y = x y=x . This visualization is quantified by the binned ECE: ECE ^ = ∑ k = 1 K | B k | n ​ | acc ​ ( B k ) − conf ​ ( B k ) | . \widehat{\mathrm{ECE}}=\sum_{k=1}^{K}\frac{|B_{k}|}{n}\left|\mathrm{acc}(B_{k})-\mathrm{conf}(B_{k})\right|. (23) It is crucial to emphasize that ECE ^ \widehat{\mathrm{ECE}} is a discretized diagnostic metric highly dependent on the chosen binning scheme. Within the CPA framework, it serves as a comparative diagnostic tool to verify calibration quality, rather than a distribution-free theoretical certificate.

sections.heading: D.1Setting A: Linear, Homoscedastic
sections.text: The covariates are generated as X i , j ∼ i.i.d. 𝒩 ​ ( 0 , 1 ) X_{i,j}\sim_{\text{i.i.d.}}\mathcal{N}(0,1) . The response Y i Y_{i} is generated from a sparse linear model: Y i = X i ⊤ ​ β + ϵ i , ϵ i ∼ 𝒩 ​ ( 0 , 1 ) . Y_{i}=X_{i}^{\top}\beta+\epsilon_{i},\quad\epsilon_{i}\sim\mathcal{N}(0,1). (24) The coefficient vector β ∈ ℝ 10 \beta\in\mathbb{R}^{10} is sparse, with the first 5 components set to 1 and the remaining 5 components set to 0.

sections.heading: D.2Setting B: Nonlinear, Heavy-Tailed
sections.text: To introduce model misspecification and test robustness against distributional violations, the response is generated as a complex nonlinear function with interactions: Y i = sin ⁡ ( 2 ​ π ​ X i , 1 ) + 2 ​ cos ⁡ ( π ​ X i , 2 ) + 3 ​ X i , 3 ​ X i , 4 + X i , 5 + ϵ i , Y_{i}=\sin(2\pi X_{i,1})+2\cos(\pi X_{i,2})+3X_{i,3}X_{i,4}+X_{i,5}+\epsilon_{i}, (25) where the noise term ϵ i ∼ t 2 \epsilon_{i}\sim t_{2} follows a Student’s t t -distribution with 2 degrees of freedom. This heavy-tailed noise distribution implies infinite variance, posing a significant challenge for standard uncertainty quantification methods.

sections.heading: D.3Setting C: Heteroscedastic
sections.text: This setting is explicitly designed to evaluate the adaptivity of CP methods to local uncertainty. The mean function is linear, but the noise variance is a function of the covariates: Y i = X i ⊤ ​ β + ϵ i , ϵ i ∼ 𝒩 ​ ( 0 , σ ​ ( X i ) 2 ) , Y_{i}=X_{i}^{\top}\beta+\epsilon_{i},\quad\epsilon_{i}\sim\mathcal{N}(0,\sigma(X_{i})^{2}), (26) where the conditional standard deviation is given by σ ​ ( X i ) = exp ⁡ ( 0.5 ​ X i , 1 ) \sigma(X_{i})=\exp(0.5X_{i,1}) . The coefficients β \beta are the same as in Setting A.

sections.text:
Let p p be the feature dimension. We draw a 5 5 -sparse coefficient vector β ∈ ℝ p \beta\in\mathbb{R}^{p} by sampling a support set S ⊂ { 1 , … , p } S\subset\{1,\dots,p\} uniformly without replacement with | S | = 5 |S|=5 , and setting β j = 𝟙 ​ { j ∈ S } \beta_{j}=\mathbbm{1}\{j\in S\} .

For each sample i i , generate raw features X ~ i , j \tilde{X}_{i,j} independently from an equal-weight mixture: X ~ i , j ∼ 1 3 ​ 𝒩 ​ ( 0 , 1 ) + 1 3 ​ SN ​ ( shape = 5 , loc = 0 , scale = 1 ) + 1 3 ​ Bern ​ ( 0.5 ) , \tilde{X}_{i,j}\sim\tfrac{1}{3}\mathcal{N}(0,1)+\tfrac{1}{3}\,\mathrm{SN}(\text{shape}=5,\text{loc}=0,\text{scale}=1)+\tfrac{1}{3}\,\mathrm{Bern}(0.5), where Bern ​ ( 0.5 ) \mathrm{Bern}(0.5) takes values in { 0 , 1 } \{0,1\} . We then induce within-row dependence by the recursion (computed in increasing j j with initialization X = X ~ X=\tilde{X} and clamping indices below 1 1 ): X i , j = 0.7 ​ X ~ i , j + 0.3 ​ X i , max ⁡ ( 1 , j − 3 ) , j = 1 , … , p . X_{i,j}=0.7\,\tilde{X}_{i,j}+0.3\,X_{i,\max(1,j-3)},\qquad j=1,\dots,p.

Define μ i = X i ⊤ ​ β \mu_{i}=X_{i}^{\top}\beta . The heteroscedastic scale is σ i = 1 + 2 ​ | μ i | 3 𝔼 ​ ( | X ⊤ ​ β | 3 ) , \sigma_{i}=1+2\frac{|\mu_{i}|^{3}}{\mathbb{E}(|X^{\top}\beta|^{3})}, where 𝔼 ​ ( | X ⊤ ​ β | 3 ) \mathbb{E}(|X^{\top}\beta|^{3}) is approximated by Monte Carlo using 10 , 000 10{,}000 auxiliary draws from the same covariate model (with a 10 − 9 10^{-9} stabilizer). Responses follow Y i = μ i + σ i ​ ξ i , ξ i ∼ i . i . d . t 2 . Y_{i}=\mu_{i}+\sigma_{i}\,\xi_{i},\qquad\xi_{i}\stackrel{{\scriptstyle i.i.d.}}{{\sim}}t_{2}. For testing, we additionally generate R R i.i.d. replicates { Y i , r } r = 1 R \{Y_{i,r}\}_{r=1}^{R} per fixed test covariate X i X_{i} using the same μ i , σ i \mu_{i},\sigma_{i} .

sections.heading: E.1Implementation Details of Classical Statistical Baselines
sections.text: We first outline the classical statistical baselines. Each method serves as a tailored parametric or asymptotic oracle for a specific DGP setting, allowing us to establish a robust traditional benchmark against which the conformal assessment framework can be evaluated. All classical routines are implemented utilizing the statsmodels and scikit-learn libraries in Python.

sections.heading: E.2Adaptive Residual Bootstrap
sections.text:
While conformal prediction offers finite-sample guarantees, traditional bootstrap methods remain a powerful frequentist benchmark for asymptotic validity. We implemented an Adaptive Residual Bootstrap procedure that dynamically selects the underlying regression model to match the data structure.

Methodology. The procedure consists of three stages: model selection, residual calibration, and bootstrap aggregation. 1. Adaptive Model Selection: To avoid specifying a functional form (linear vs. nonlinear) a priori, we evaluate two candidate learners on the training set 𝒟 train \mathcal{D}_{\text{train}} : a linear Lasso and a nonlinear RandomForest . The optimal base learner f ^ \hat{f} is selected via 5-fold Cross-Validation (CV) based on the lowest Mean Squared Error (MSE). 2. Residual Calibration: Standard in-sample residuals often underestimate prediction error. We explicitly compute out-of-sample residuals r i = y i − f ^ − k ​ ( i ) ​ ( x i ) r_{i}=y_{i}-\hat{f}^{-k(i)}(x_{i}) using K K -fold CV, where f ^ − k ​ ( i ) \hat{f}^{-k(i)} is trained on folds excluding i i . These residuals are then centered: r ~ i = r i − r ¯ \tilde{r}_{i}=r_{i}-\bar{r} . 3. Bootstrap Inference: We generate B = 500 B=500 bootstrap replications. In each iteration b b : • A bootstrap sample 𝒟 b ∗ \mathcal{D}^{*}_{b} is drawn from 𝒟 train \mathcal{D}_{\text{train}} with replacement. • The selected estimator is re-trained on 𝒟 b ∗ \mathcal{D}^{*}_{b} to obtain f ^ b ∗ \hat{f}^{*}_{b} . • For a test point x n + 1 x_{n+1} , we simulate the predictive distribution as y b ∗ = f ^ b ∗ ​ ( x n + 1 ) + ϵ b ∗ y^{*}_{b}=\hat{f}^{*}_{b}(x_{n+1})+\epsilon^{*}_{b} , where ϵ b ∗ \epsilon^{*}_{b} is drawn with replacement from the centered residuals { r ~ i } \{\tilde{r}_{i}\} . The ( 1 − α ) (1-\alpha) prediction interval is given by the empirical α / 2 \alpha/2 and 1 − α / 2 1-\alpha/2 quantiles of { y b ∗ } b = 1 B \{y^{*}_{b}\}_{b=1}^{B} .

Implementation Details. • Hyperparameter Tuning: For Random Forest, we performed a grid search over n_estimators ∈ { 200 , 500 } \in\{200,500\} , max_depth ∈ { 5 , 10 , None } \in\{5,10,\text{None}\} , min_samples_split ∈ { 2 , 5 } \in\{2,5\} , and max_features ∈ { sqrt , log2 } \in\{\text{sqrt},\text{log2}\} . For Lasso, the regularization path was automatically tuned via LassoCV . • Computation: The bootstrap loop was parallelized using Python’s multiprocessing module to ensure efficiency. We fixed B = 500 B=500 to balance computational cost and quantile estimation stability.

sections.heading: E.3Quantile Regression Forests (QRF)
sections.text:
To estimate conditional prediction intervals without relying on linear assumptions or explicit residual modeling, we implemented Quantile Regression Forests. QRF generalizes random forests by estimating the full conditional distribution P ​ ( Y | X = x ) P(Y|X=x) rather than just the conditional mean.

Methodology The method operates in two phases: structural learning and distribution estimation. 1. Tree Structure Learning: A standard random forest of B B trees is trained to minimize the Mean Squared Error. For a given input x x , let L b ​ ( x ) L_{b}(x) denote the set of training indices falling into the same leaf as x x in the b b -th tree. 2. Conditional Distribution: Instead of averaging the leaf means, QRF aggregates the observed response values from all leaves associated with x x . The estimated conditional distribution function is given by: F ^ ​ ( y | x ) = 1 B ​ ∑ b = 1 B ∑ i ∈ L b ​ ( x ) 1 | L b ​ ( x ) | ​ 𝟙 { Y i ≤ y } . \hat{F}(y|x)=\frac{1}{B}\sum_{b=1}^{B}\sum_{i\in L_{b}(x)}\frac{1}{|L_{b}(x)|}\mathbbm{1}_{\{Y_{i}\leq y\}}. (27) In our implementation, this is approximated by collecting the ensemble of target values 𝒴 x = ⋃ b = 1 B { Y i : i ∈ L b ​ ( x ) } \mathcal{Y}_{x}=\bigcup_{b=1}^{B}\{Y_{i}:i\in L_{b}(x)\} and computing empirical quantiles. 3. Interval Construction: The prediction interval is defined as [ Q ^ α / 2 ​ ( 𝒴 x ) , Q ^ 1 − α / 2 ​ ( 𝒴 x ) ] [\hat{Q}_{\alpha/2}(\mathcal{Y}_{x}),\hat{Q}_{1-\alpha/2}(\mathcal{Y}_{x})] , where Q ^ τ \hat{Q}_{\tau} is the τ \tau -quantile of the collected values.

Implementation Details • Hyperparameter Optimization: We tuned the random forest structure using 5-fold GridSearchCV on the training set. The search space included n_estimators ∈ { 200 , 500 } \in\{200,500\} , max_depth ∈ { 5 , 10 } \in\{5,10\} , and min_samples_leaf ∈ { 5 , 10 } \in\{5,10\} . The latter parameter is critical as it controls the sample size available for local distribution estimation. • Algorithm: We utilized scikit-learn ’s RandomForestRegressor to grow the trees and its .apply() method to retrieve leaf indices. The conditional quantiles were computed using numpy.quantile on the aggregated leaf samples.

sections.heading: E.4Split Conformal Prediction with Adaptive Selection
sections.text:
We implemented the standard Split Conformal Prediction (SCP) method (Lei et al. , 2018 ) augmented with an adaptive model selection step. This approach guarantees finite-sample marginal coverage regardless of the underlying data distribution, provided the observations are exchangeable.

Methodology. The procedure involves a three-way data split: a training set 𝒟 train \mathcal{D}_{\text{train}} for model learning, a calibration set 𝒟 calib \mathcal{D}_{\text{calib}} for score computation, and a test set for evaluation. 1. Adaptive Model Learning: To accommodate unknown DGPs ranging from linear to highly nonlinear, we perform model selection on 𝒟 train \mathcal{D}_{\text{train}} . We evaluate two candidate learners: Lasso (linear baseline) and RandomForest (nonlinear baseline). The optimal regressor μ ^ \hat{\mu} is selected based on the minimum 5-fold Cross-Validation MSE. 2. Calibration: We compute the non-conformity scores on the hold-out calibration set 𝒟 calib \mathcal{D}_{\text{calib}} as the absolute prediction errors: S i = | y i − μ ^ ​ ( x i ) | S_{i}=|y_{i}-\hat{\mu}(x_{i})| . 3. Interval Construction: Let q ^ 1 − α \hat{q}_{1-\alpha} be the ( 1 − α ) (1-\alpha) -quantile of the calibration scores { S i } \{S_{i}\} . The prediction interval for a new point x x is constructed as 𝒞 ​ ( x ) = [ μ ^ ​ ( x ) − q ^ 1 − α , μ ^ ​ ( x ) + q ^ 1 − α ] \mathcal{C}(x)=[\hat{\mu}(x)-\hat{q}_{1-\alpha},\hat{\mu}(x)+\hat{q}_{1-\alpha}] . This results in intervals of constant width across the feature space.

Implementation Details. • Software: The conformalization process was implemented using the MAPIE library in Python, utilizing the cv="prefit" mode to strictly separate training and calibration data. • Hyperparameters: The RandomForest candidate was tuned over a grid of n_estimators ∈ { 200 , 500 } \in\{200,500\} and max_depth ∈ { 5 , 10 , None } \in\{5,10,\text{None}\} . The Lasso regularization path was automatically optimized via internal cross-validation. • Data Splitting: We used an equal split ratio between training and calibration sets ( ρ calib = 0.5 \rho_{\text{calib}}=0.5 ) to balance estimation quality and calibration stability.

sections.heading: E.5Locally Adaptive (Studentized) Split Conformal Prediction
sections.text:
Standard split conformal prediction produces intervals of constant width, which fail to reflect local uncertainty in heteroscedastic data. To address this, we implemented the Locally Adaptive Split Conformal Prediction method, often referred to as Studentized Conformal Prediction.

Methodology. This approach normalizes the non-conformity score by an estimate of the local conditional dispersion. The procedure consists of two training steps on the proper training set 𝒟 train \mathcal{D}_{\text{train}} : 1. Mean Estimator: A primary regression model μ ^ \hat{\mu} is trained to predict the conditional mean 𝔼 ​ [ Y | X ] \mathbb{E}[Y|X] . 2. Dispersion Estimator: We compute the absolute residuals on the training set, R i = | Y i − μ ^ ​ ( X i ) | R_{i}=|Y_{i}-\hat{\mu}(X_{i})| . A secondary regression model σ ^ \hat{\sigma} is then trained on { ( X i , R i ) } \{(X_{i},R_{i})\} to predict these residuals, effectively estimating the local mean absolute deviation. 3. Studentized Calibration: On the calibration set 𝒟 calib \mathcal{D}_{\text{calib}} , we compute the normalized non-conformity scores: S i = | Y i − μ ^ ​ ( X i ) | σ ^ ​ ( X i ) + ϵ , S_{i}=\frac{|Y_{i}-\hat{\mu}(X_{i})|}{\hat{\sigma}(X_{i})+\epsilon}, (28) where ϵ = 10 − 6 \epsilon=10^{-6} is a small constant added for numerical stability. 4. Interval Construction: Let Q ^ 1 − α \hat{Q}_{1-\alpha} be the ( 1 − α ) (1-\alpha) -quantile of the scores { S i } \{S_{i}\} . The prediction interval for a new point x x scales with the estimated difficulty: 𝒞 ​ ( x ) = μ ^ ​ ( x ) ± Q ^ 1 − α ⋅ σ ^ ​ ( x ) \mathcal{C}(x)=\hat{\mu}(x)\pm\hat{Q}_{1-\alpha}\cdot\hat{\sigma}(x) .

Implementation Details • Base Learners: Both μ ^ \hat{\mu} and σ ^ \hat{\sigma} were instantiated as RandomForestRegressor models to capture nonlinear patterns in both the central tendency and the variability. • Optimization: We performed independent 3-fold Cross-Validation Grid Searches for both models to optimize their hyperparameters. The search grid included n_estimators ∈ { 200 , 500 } \in\{200,500\} , max_depth ∈ { 5 , 10 , None } \in\{5,10,\text{None}\} , and min_samples_split ∈ { 2 , 5 } \in\{2,5\} . • Data Splitting: Consistent with the standard split conformal approach, we maintained disjoint training and calibration sets.

sections.heading: E.6Conformalized Quantile Regression (CQR)
sections.text:
To construct intervals that adapt to both the location and spread of the data distribution, we employed Conformalized Quantile Regression (CQR) (Romano et al. , 2019 ) . CQR calibrates initial estimates of the conditional quantiles to guarantee validity.

Methodology. The method proceeds in two steps: 1. Quantile Estimation: We estimate the lower ( α / 2 \alpha/2 ) and upper ( 1 − α / 2 1-\alpha/2 ) conditional quantiles using a regression model trained on 𝒟 train \mathcal{D}_{\text{train}} . To robustly handle diverse DGPs, we implemented an adaptive selection strategy that chooses between a linear QuantileRegressor and a nonlinear GradientBoostingRegressor based on cross-validation performance. 2. Conformal Calibration: We compute non-conformity scores on a calibration set as S i = max ⁡ ( q ^ α / 2 ​ ( X i ) − Y i , Y i − q ^ 1 − α / 2 ​ ( X i ) ) S_{i}=\max(\hat{q}_{\alpha/2}(X_{i})-Y_{i},\;Y_{i}-\hat{q}_{1-\alpha/2}(X_{i})) . The final interval is constructed by expanding (or contracting) the initial quantile estimates by the ( 1 − α ) (1-\alpha) -quantile of these scores: 𝒞 ​ ( x ) = [ q ^ α / 2 ​ ( x ) − Q 1 − α , q ^ 1 − α / 2 ​ ( x ) + Q 1 − α ] \mathcal{C}(x)=[\hat{q}_{\alpha/2}(x)-Q_{1-\alpha},\hat{q}_{1-\alpha/2}(x)+Q_{1-\alpha}] .

Implementation Details • Base Learners: The linear candidate employed L1-regularization, tuned over λ ∈ { 10 − 3 , 10 − 2 , 0.1 , 1 } \lambda\in\{10^{-3},10^{-2},0.1,1\} . The gradient boosting candidate was optimized over n_estimators ∈ { 100 , 200 , 300 } \in\{100,200,300\} , max_depth ∈ { 3 , 5 , 7 } \in\{3,5,7\} , and learning_rate ∈ { 0.01 , 0.05 , 0.1 } \in\{0.01,0.05,0.1\} . • Software: We utilized the MapieQuantileRegressor from the MAPIE library to perform the calibration step.

sections.heading: E.7Cross-Validation Plus (CV+)
sections.text:
To overcome the sample efficiency limitations of split conformal prediction without incurring the high computational cost of full jackknife (leave-one-out) methods, we employed the Cross-Validation Plus (CV+) method (Barber et al. , 2021 ) .

Methodology. CV+ constructs prediction intervals using the empirical distribution of leave-one-fold-out residuals. 1. Base Model Selection: Similar to the split conformal approach, we first select the optimal base learner μ ^ \hat{\mu} (Lasso or Random Forest) via 5-fold cross-validation on the training set. 2. K-Fold Training: The training data is partitioned into K K disjoint folds S 1 , … , S K S_{1},\dots,S_{K} . For each k ∈ { 1 , … , K } k\in\{1,\dots,K\} , a model μ ^ − k \hat{\mu}_{-k} is trained on the data excluding fold S k S_{k} . 3. Residual Computation: For each training sample i i belonging to fold S k ​ ( i ) S_{k(i)} , we compute the absolute out-of-fold residual R i = | y i − μ ^ − k ​ ( i ) ​ ( x i ) | R_{i}=|y_{i}-\hat{\mu}_{-k(i)}(x_{i})| . 4. Interval Construction: For a test point x x , the prediction interval is constructed by taking empirical quantiles of the shifted predictions: 𝒞 CV+ ( x ) = [ Quantile α { μ ^ − k ​ ( i ) ( x ) − R i } i = 1 n , Quantile 1 − α { μ ^ − k ​ ( i ) ( x ) + R i } i = 1 n ] . \mathcal{C}_{\text{CV+}}(x)=\Big[\operatorname{Quantile}_{\alpha}\big\{\hat{\mu}_{-k(i)}(x)-R_{i}\big\}_{i=1}^{n},\;\operatorname{Quantile}_{1-\alpha}\big\{\hat{\mu}_{-k(i)}(x)+R_{i}\big\}_{i=1}^{n}\Big].

Implementation Details • Algorithm: We utilized the MapieRegressor with method="plus" and cv=5 . This K = 5 K=5 configuration balances coverage validity with computational feasibility. • Hyperparameters: The base estimators were tuned over the same grid as in the split conformal experiments ( n_estimators ∈ { 200 , 500 } \in\{200,500\} for RF).

sections.heading: E.8Localized Conformal Prediction (LCP)
sections.text:
To adapt to local heteroscedasticity without the randomness of data splitting or auxiliary variance modeling, we implemented Localized Conformal Prediction (Guan, 2023 ) .

Methodology. LCP modifies the standard conformal score integration by re-weighting calibration samples based on their proximity to the test point. 1. Local Weighting: Let μ ^ \hat{\mu} be the base regression model. For a test point x x , we assign a weight to each calibration residual S i = | y i − μ ^ ​ ( X i ) | S_{i}=|y_{i}-\hat{\mu}(X_{i})| using a Gaussian kernel: w h ​ ( x , X i ) = exp ⁡ ( − ‖ x − X i ‖ 2 2 h ) . w_{h}(x,X_{i})=\exp\left(-\frac{\|x-X_{i}\|_{2}^{2}}{h}\right). (29) 2. Bandwidth Selection: The performance of LCP is sensitive to the kernel bandwidth h h . We implemented the data-driven autoTune procedure described in Guan ( 2023 ) . This routine performs bootstrap resampling on the training data to select the h h that minimizes the deviation between empirical coverage and the target level 1 − α 1-\alpha . 3. Inference: The prediction interval is constructed using the weighted quantile of the calibration scores, where the probability mass of each score S i S_{i} is proportional to w h ​ ( x , X i ) w_{h}(x,X_{i}) .

Implementation Details • Base Learner: A RandomForestRegressor (200 trees) trained on 𝒟 train \mathcal{D}_{\text{train}} . • Optimization: To accelerate the computationally intensive re-weighting step for every test point, we vectorized the distance computations using scipy.spatial.distance.cdist and parallelized the quantile search using joblib .

sections.heading: E.9Randomized Local Conformal Prediction (RLCP)
sections.text:
Standard LCP does not guarantee finite-sample marginal coverage in all settings. To address this theoretical limitation while maintaining local adaptivity, we implemented Randomized LCP (RLCP) (Hore and Barber, 2025 ) .

Methodology. RLCP introduces a randomization mechanism to the weight calculation to restore exchangeability properties. 1. Noise Injection: Instead of using the exact distance ‖ x − X i ‖ \|x-X_{i}\| , RLCP computes weights based on perturbed locations. We specifically implemented the Gaussian smoothing kernel variant. For a test point x x , we generate random noise vectors ξ ∼ 𝒩 ​ ( 0 , h 2 ​ I ) \xi\sim\mathcal{N}(0,h^{2}I) . 2. Smoothed Weights: The weights are computed as w ​ ( x , X i ) = exp ⁡ ( − ‖ x + ξ − X i ‖ 2 / 2 ​ h 2 ) w(x,X_{i})=\exp(-\|x+\xi-X_{i}\|^{2}/2h^{2}) . In our implementation, we utilized the m-RLCP algorithm, which averages the threshold over m m independent noise realizations to reduce the variance of the interval boundaries. 3. Bandwidth Selection: Unlike LCP, for RLCP we employed a computational heuristic for bandwidth selection to ensure scalability. We set h h to the median distance between each calibration point and its k k -nearest neighbors in the training set, with k = n train k=\sqrt{n_{\text{train}}} .

Implementation Details • Parameters: We set the number of noise realizations to m = 10 m=10 . • Computation: The neighborhood search for bandwidth selection was implemented using sklearn.neighbors.NearestNeighbors . The noise injection and threshold averaging were parallelized across CPU cores using a dynamic batching strategy.

sections.heading: F.1Distance-Weighted Kendall’s Tau (τw\tau_{w})
sections.text:
Standard rank correlation metrics treat all transpositions equally. In the context of model selection, penalizing the transposition of two models with nearly identical performance is overly harsh, whereas failing to distinguish between a highly accurate model and a severely miscalibrated one constitutes a critical failure. Therefore, we adopt a Distance-Weighted Kendall’s τ \tau , which assigns penalties proportional to the actual performance gap.

Let ( i , j ) (i,j) denote a pair of algorithms, and d i , d j d_{i},d_{j} be their ground-truth conditional validity errors. We define the indicator of concordance as K i ​ j = sgn ⁡ ( π ​ ( i ) − π ​ ( j ) ) ⋅ sgn ⁡ ( π ^ ​ ( i ) − π ^ ​ ( j ) ) K_{ij}=\operatorname{sgn}(\pi(i)-\pi(j))\cdot\operatorname{sgn}(\hat{\pi}(i)-\hat{\pi}(j)) . The weighted correlation is computed as: τ w ​ ( π , π ^ ) = ∑ 1 ≤ i < j ≤ M w i ​ j ​ K i ​ j ∑ 1 ≤ i < j ≤ M w i ​ j , \tau_{w}(\pi,\hat{\pi})=\frac{\sum_{1\leq i<j\leq M}w_{ij}K_{ij}}{\sum_{1\leq i<j\leq M}w_{ij}}, (30) where the weight w i ​ j = | d i − d j | p w_{ij}=|d_{i}-d_{j}|^{p} (with p = 1 p=1 in our experiments) ensures that pairs with larger performance disparities exert a greater influence on the correlation score.

sections.heading: F.2Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain (NDCG)
sections.text: While Kendall’s τ w \tau_{w} measures general pairwise correlation, NDCG quantifies the utility of the selection, strictly rewarding the estimator for placing high-quality models at the top of the list. The metric at cutoff k k is defined as: NDCG ​ @ ​ k = DCG ​ @ ​ k IDCG ​ @ ​ k , with DCG ​ @ ​ k = ∑ p = 1 k rel ​ ( π ^ − 1 ​ ( p ) ) log 2 ⁡ ( p + 1 ) , \text{NDCG}@k=\frac{\text{DCG}@k}{\text{IDCG}@k},\quad\text{with }\text{DCG}@k=\sum_{p=1}^{k}\frac{\text{rel}(\hat{\pi}^{-1}(p))}{\log_{2}(p+1)}, (31) where π ^ − 1 ​ ( p ) \hat{\pi}^{-1}(p) denotes the item at rank p p in the estimated list. To prevent numerical instability associated with reciprocal transformations, the relevance score is derived using a linear inversion of the true distance: rel ​ ( i ) = max j ⁡ ( d j ) − d i \text{rel}(i)=\max_{j}(d_{j})-d_{i} . This mapping ensures that models with smaller true errors naturally yield proportionally higher utility gains. IDCG is the ideal gain achieved by the perfect Oracle permutation.

sections.heading: F.3Hit Rate (Hit@k)
sections.text: To directly assess the success rate of identifying the optimal subset of algorithms, we use the Hit Rate. This metric calculates the overlap proportion between the top- k k sets identified by the Oracle ( 𝒮 oracle ( k ) \mathcal{S}_{\text{oracle}}^{(k)} ) and the CPA estimator ( 𝒮 est ( k ) \mathcal{S}_{\text{est}}^{(k)} ): Hit ​ @ ​ k = | 𝒮 oracle ( k ) ∩ 𝒮 est ( k ) | k . \text{Hit}@k=\frac{|\mathcal{S}_{\text{oracle}}^{(k)}\cap\mathcal{S}_{\text{est}}^{(k)}|}{k}. (32) A Hit@k of 1 implies that the CPA framework successfully retrieved all k k best-performing models, regardless of their internal relative order.

sections.heading: G.1The Automated Model Selection Procedure (Baseline)
sections.text:
The Baseline reliability estimator utilizes a rigorous automated machine learning (AutoML) pipeline to identify the optimal probabilistic classifier for conditional coverage estimation. Given that standard target marginal coverage levels (e.g., 1 − α = 0.9 1-\alpha=0.9 ) inherently induce severe class imbalance within the coverage indicators, all candidate estimators are trained utilizing inverse class-frequency weighting. The pipeline consists of three sequential stages:

1. Hypothesis Space Definition. We construct a diverse pool of classification families to capture varying degrees of nonlinearity and feature interactions. For each family, we optimize over a pre-defined hyperparameter grid Θ \Theta : • Logistic Regression (LR): ⋅ \cdot Regularization penalty: ℓ 1 , ℓ 2 \ell_{1},\ell_{2} ⋅ \cdot Inverse regularization strength C C : { 0.01 , 0.1 , 1 , 10 , 100 } \{0.01,0.1,1,10,100\} ⋅ \cdot Solvers: { liblinear , saga } \{\text{liblinear},\text{saga}\} • Random Forest (RF): ⋅ \cdot Number of estimators B B : { 100 , 200 , 500 } \{100,200,500\} ⋅ \cdot Maximum depth d d : { None , 5 , 10 } \{\text{None},5,10\} ⋅ \cdot Minimum samples split: { 2 , 5 } \{2,5\} ⋅ \cdot Max features: { p , log 2 ⁡ p } \{\sqrt{p},\log_{2}p\} • Gradient Boosting Machine (GBM): ⋅ \cdot Number of estimators B B : { 100 , 200 , 300 } \{100,200,300\} ⋅ \cdot Learning rate η \eta : { 0.01 , 0.05 , 0.1 } \{0.01,0.05,0.1\} ⋅ \cdot Subsampling rate: { 0.8 , 1.0 } \{0.8,1.0\} ⋅ \cdot Minimum samples split: { 2 , 5 } \{2,5\} • XGBoost (XGB): ⋅ \cdot Number of estimators B B : { 100 , 200 } \{100,200\} ⋅ \cdot Maximum depth d d : { 3 , 5 , 7 } \{3,5,7\} ⋅ \cdot Learning rate η \eta : { 0.01 , 0.1 } \{0.01,0.1\} ⋅ \cdot Subsample & Colsample by tree: { 0.8 , 1.0 } \{0.8,1.0\} • K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN): ⋅ \cdot Neighbors k k : { 3 , 5 , 10 , 30 } \{3,5,10,30\} ⋅ \cdot Weighting: { uniform , distance } \{\text{uniform},\text{distance}\} ⋅ \cdot Metric: { Minkowski ( p = 1 , p = 2 ) , Euclidean } \{\text{Minkowski ($p=1,p=2$)},\text{Euclidean}\} 2. Estimator Selection via Cross-Validation. Candidate models are evaluated using stratified K K -fold cross-validation on the hold-out evaluation set 𝒟 eval \mathcal{D}_{\text{eval}} . To maintain sufficient minority class representation at extreme quantiles ( α ≤ 0.02 \alpha\leq 0.02 ), the number of folds K K is dynamically adjusted from 5 to 2. The configuration f ^ base \hat{f}_{\text{base}} that minimizes the cross-validated negative log-likelihood (log-loss) is selected as the optimal base estimator. 3. Non-parametric Calibration. High-capacity classifiers often produce uncalibrated probability estimates. To rectify this without distorting the learned ranking, we apply Isotonic Regression (Zadrozny and Elkan, 2002 ) via cross-fitting. This step fits a monotonically non-decreasing piecewise constant function to the raw scores, yielding the final calibrated reliability estimates η ^ ​ ( x ) \hat{\eta}(x) .

sections.heading: G.2Perturbation Configurations
sections.text:
To rigorously evaluate the robustness of the proposed conformal assessment framework, we introduce seven structural and procedural perturbations to the reliability estimator. These configurations are meticulously designed to simulate a spectrum of sub-optimal estimation behaviors, ranging from absent calibration to severe capacity constraints. The exact specifications, derived directly from our evaluation pipeline, are detailed in Table 4 .

Rationale for Perturbations. Configurations pert_1 through pert_3 isolate the impact of the post-hoc calibration phase, contrasting the baseline isotonic approach with raw uncalibrated probabilities, parametric scaling, and discrete histogram binning. Configuration pert_4 restricts the hypothesis space entirely to linear decision boundaries, acting as a structural stress test under nonlinear data generating processes. Configurations pert_5 and pert_6 bypass the cross-validation tuning phase, imposing strict architectural bottlenecks on tree ensembles to simulate under-parameterized regimes. Finally, pert_7 serves as a competent, single-family baseline to evaluate whether the heterogeneous AutoML ensemble search strictly outperforms a well-tuned classical boosting algorithm.

sections.heading: I.1The Bias-Variance Trade-off
sections.text:
A critical decision in deploying the CPA framework is the partitioning of the available labeled data 𝒟 \mathcal{D} ( | 𝒟 | = N |\mathcal{D}|=N ) into a training set for the conformal predictor ( n train n_{\text{train}} ) and an evaluation set for the reliability estimator ( n eval n_{\text{eval}} ). Let ρ = n train / N \rho=n_{\text{train}}/N denote the split ratio. This allocation is governed by the bias-variance trade-off formalized in Section 3 :

• Approximation Error ( ρ → 0 \rho\to 0 ): As the training set shrinks, the conformal predictor is trained on insufficient data. Its conditional coverage properties may diverge from its asymptotic behavior on the full dataset. • Estimation Error ( ρ → 1 \rho\to 1 ): As the evaluation set shrinks, the reliability estimator η ^ \hat{\eta} is constrained by data scarcity, preventing it from effectively learning the decision boundary of the coverage indicator. This results in high-variance estimates of conditional validity.

sections.heading: I.2Empirical Results
sections.text:
To empirically characterize this trade-off, we evaluated the ranking consistency (Weighted Kendall’s τ w \tau_{w} ) across all four DGPs varying ρ ∈ { 0.1 , 0.3 , 0.5 , 0.7 , 0.9 } \rho\in\{0.1,0.3,0.5,0.7,0.9\} . Figure 10 presents the resulting performance trajectories.

The results generally exhibit a distinct concave profile , particularly for the complex scenarios (Settings B, C, and D). In these regimes, extreme splits ( ρ = 0.1 \rho=0.1 or ρ = 0.9 \rho=0.9 ) lead to a degradation in ranking correlation, confirming that limiting the sample size for either the subject model or the assessor compromises the overall evaluation fidelity.

Performance consistently peaks in the balanced regime ( ρ ≈ 0.5 \rho\approx 0.5 ). However, Setting A presents a notable exception, where performance is maximized at ρ = 0.1 \rho=0.1 . This deviation is attributable to the low sample complexity of the linear, homoscedastic data generating process; the conformal predictors converge rapidly, allowing the majority of samples to be allocated to the reliability estimator to suppress estimation variance in a low-signal environment. For real-world data where the underlying structure is unknown, an equal allocation ( ρ = 0.5 \rho=0.5 ) provides a robust heuristic to balance predictive quality and audit precision.

sections.heading: J.1Experimental Protocol
sections.text:
Our investigation focuses on the heteroscedastic regression scenario (Setting C) , representing the most demanding regime for conditional coverage auditing due to the covariate-dependent noise structure. We adopt a rigorous “Selection-Deployment” protocol:

• Data Configuration: We systematically vary the total budget N ∈ [ 200 , 2000 ] N\in[200,2000] across different feature dimensionalities p ∈ { 10 , 20 , 50 } p\in\{10,20,50\} . • CPA Implementation: Following our heuristic recommendations, we employ a balanced split ( ρ = 0.5 \rho=0.5 ). The reliability estimator η ^ ​ ( x ) \hat{\eta}(x) is trained via the AutoML pipeline described in Appendix G to ensure maximally flexible modeling of the coverage boundary. • Oracle Benchmark: Ground-truth rankings are derived by evaluating all candidate methods on the full data distribution using a large independent test set ( N test = 2000 N_{\text{test}}=2000 ) and the known DGP parameters. • Performance Metrics: Assessment fidelity is quantified via Weighted Kendall’s τ w \tau_{w} (ranking alignment), Hit@1 (precision of best-model identification), and NDCG@1 (selection utility).

sections.heading: J.2Results and Analysis
sections.text:
Figure 11 illustrates the empirical performance trajectories of the CPA framework as a function of the total sample size N N .

1. Threshold Behavior and Recommended Sample Size. The primary objective of this simulation is to identify the critical sample size required for statistically meaningful evaluations. Panel A reveals a clear threshold behavior in ranking consistency ( τ w \tau_{w} ). In the limited-sample regime ( N < 400 N<400 ), estimation variance dominates the signal. As N N surpasses 600, the signal-to-noise ratio improves sufficiently to resolve structural coverage gaps, with τ w \tau_{w} stabilizing above 0.5 at N = 1000 N=1000 . Based on these findings, we recommend practitioners allocate a minimum of n eval ≥ 400 n_{\text{eval}}\geq 400 evaluation samples (corresponding to N ≥ 800 N\geq 800 under a ρ = 0.5 \rho=0.5 split) to ensure a robust and reliable CPA audit.

2. Dimensionality Modulates the Threshold. Panel B indicates that feature dimensionality shifts this empirical threshold. Lower-dimensional settings ( p = 10 p=10 ) converge earlier, whereas higher-dimensional spaces ( p = 50 p=50 ) demand larger sample sizes to initiate accurate identification. Nevertheless, once the sample complexity requirement is satisfied ( N ≥ 1500 N\geq 1500 ), higher dimensions ultimately yield superior identification accuracy, as the true performance disparities between adaptive and rigid methods become statistically discernible.

3. Utility Under Sub-optimal Sample Sizes. Finally, Panel C highlights the framework’s robustness under finite-sample constraints. Even below the recommended threshold ( N ≈ 600 N\approx 600 ), where global ranking consistency is still developing, NDCG@1 scores reliably exceed 0.75. This confirms that while the framework may lack the precision to perfectly rank all candidates given limited data, it retains sufficient discriminatory power to consistently isolate high-utility models and discard severe under-performers.

sections.heading: K.1Worst Slab Coverage
sections.text:
We provide additional details on the Worst Slab Coverage (WSC) metric, originally proposed to assess conditional-like coverage properties beyond marginal guarantees. Let X ∈ ℝ d X\in\mathbb{R}^{d} denote the feature vector and Y Y the response. For a given direction v ∈ ℝ d v\in\mathbb{R}^{d} and real numbers a < b a<b , define a slab S v , a , b := { x ∈ ℝ d : a ≤ v ⊤ ​ x ≤ b } . S_{v,a,b}:=\{x\in\mathbb{R}^{d}:a\leq v^{\top}x\leq b\}. Slabs provide a flexible yet computationally tractable family of subsets of the feature space, allowing us to probe coverage behavior along low-dimensional projections of X X . Given a prediction or confidence set C ^ ​ ( ⋅ ) \widehat{C}(\cdot) and a threshold 0 < δ ≤ 1 0<\delta\leq 1 , the worst slab coverage along direction v v is defined as WSC n ( C ^ , v ) := inf a < b { P n ( Y ∈ C ^ ( X ) ∣ a ≤ v ⊤ X ≤ b ) : P n ( a ≤ v ⊤ X ≤ b ) ≥ δ } , \mathrm{WSC}_{n}(\widehat{C},v):=\inf_{a<b}\left\{P_{n}\!\left(Y\in\widehat{C}(X)\mid a\leq v^{\top}X\leq b\right)\;:\;P_{n}(a\leq v^{\top}X\leq b)\geq\delta\right\}, where P n P_{n} denotes the empirical distribution of the observed sample { ( X i , Y i ) } i = 1 n \{(X_{i},Y_{i})\}_{i=1}^{n} .
Intuitively, WSC n ​ ( C ^ , v ) \mathrm{WSC}_{n}(\widehat{C},v) captures the worst-case empirical coverage over all slabs along direction v v that contain at least a δ \delta fraction of the data.

This criterion can be interpreted as an intermediate notion between marginal and full conditional coverage.
While exact conditional coverage is generally unattainable without strong assumptions, WSC detects systematic coverage failures that may occur on structured subsets of the feature space, even when marginal coverage is satisfied.

In practice, WSC is computed using a finite collection of randomly sampled directions.
For each direction, the feature vectors are projected onto a one-dimensional axis and sorted, and the empirical coverage is evaluated over all contiguous intervals in the projected space that contain at least a δ \delta fraction of the observations.
The minimum coverage over such intervals defines the worst slab coverage for that direction, and the overall WSC is obtained by taking the minimum across all sampled directions.

We consider two variants of this procedure.
The first is an in-sample (biased) version, where the same dataset is used both to identify the adversarial direction and slab and to evaluate the resulting coverage.
The second is an out-of-sample (unbiased) version based on sample splitting: the adversarial direction and slab are selected using a training subset, and the coverage is subsequently evaluated on a held-out test subset restricted to that slab.

In our experiments, we observe that the WSC metric is highly sensitive to the choice of projection directions.
As a consequence, the in-sample and out-of-sample variants can yield substantially different numerical values.
In particular, the sample-splitting (unbiased) version often produces much more conservative estimates, with WSC values substantially higher and frequently very close to the nominal target level.
While this behavior is expected to some extent due to the reduced adaptivity in the slab selection step, it may also mask localized coverage failures that WSC is designed to detect.
For this reason, we primarily rely on the in-sample (biased) WSC as a diagnostic tool in our empirical studies, as it more effectively reveals potential worst-case coverage deficiencies across directions.

sections.heading: K.3Multivariate Reliability Landscapes for Bike Sharing Demand
sections.text:
We extend the Bike Sharing analysis to explore interacting covariates in Figure 12 . The visual alignment between the empirical coverage (left column) and the CPA-estimated reliability surface (middle column) confirms that our framework reconstructs complex failure boundaries. These estimated landscapes provide a granular diagnostic comparison of the three conformal procedures. First, CP-Residual (top row) , which imposes a homoscedastic assumption, exhibits substantial undercoverage (red regions, dropping below 0.6) during rush hours (8–10 and 16–19), particularly on warm days ( > 20 ∘ ​ C >20^{\circ}\text{C} ). Conversely, it yields overcoverage (dark blue) at night (hours 0–5). Operationally, casual ridership surges on warm evenings, amplifying the conditional variance; a constant-width interval is thus conservative during dormant periods but insufficient during peak volatility. Second, CP-Studentized (middle row) scales intervals with local variance estimates. While undercoverage regions become shallower, systematic deficits persist, indicating that simple variance scaling struggles to fully capture the complex interaction between temperature and time.

Finally, CQR (bottom row) directly fits conditional quantiles, effectively adapting to the underlying heteroscedasticity. The undercoverage zones are largely mitigated, yielding a landscape that consistently hovers near the nominal target across the bivariate feature space. Crucially, these multivariate landscapes transition model assessment from aggregate marginal summaries to localized failure characterization, enabling practitioners to identify specific operational regimes that necessitate targeted interventions.
